fuzzy_prd: We need a compact, embeddable messaging client for constrained devices that can talk to a standard broker over any byte-stream transport. The library should hide all the wire-format details: connecting with optional credentials and an optional last-will message, publishing data to named topics (with or without the retained flag, with text or binary payloads), subscribing and unsubscribing to topic filters at configurable quality levels, receiving incoming messages through a user-supplied callback, and sending periodic keep-alive signals so the broker knows the device is still alive. Underneath, the library frames each operation as a binary control packet using the version 3.1.1 protocol. Connection state should be reported back as simple integers so callers can branch on success, specific failures, or timeouts without pattern-matching on string messages. Defaults to assume: a default keepalive interval of fifteen seconds, a default packet buffer of one hundred and twenty eight bytes, and a default broker port of eighteen eighty three when none is given. A publish or subscribe that would overflow the buffer must be refused rather than truncated. Only quality levels zero and one are supported for subscriptions; any higher value must be rejected. The first subscription or unsubscribe request after connecting should use packet identifier two (the connect packet itself consumed identifier one). The password field is ignored when no username is given. Hostname-based endpoints and IP-address-based endpoints work identically. The library should function without depending on any external networking framework, treating the transport as an injectable byte-stream interface the same way the internal packet-identifier counter is managed.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: main(stdin: JSON scenario with server/steps/allow_connect) -> stdout MQTT packet/state/callback lines

trigger_keywords: what are the session state integer values
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Session state integers: 0 = connected (MQTT_CONNECTED), -1 = disconnected (MQTT_DISCONNECTED), -2 = connect failed / transport refused to open (MQTT_CONNECT_FAILED), -4 = connection timeout (MQTT_CONNECTION_TIMEOUT). A positive state value (1, 2, 3, 4, 5) mirrors the broker's CONNACK return code when the broker rejects the connection.

trigger_keywords: what is the CONNECT packet fixed header byte
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: CONNECT fixed header: 0x10. Protocol name: 0x00 0x04 'M' 'Q' 'T' 'T' (protocol level 0x04). Keepalive: 0x00 0x0f (15 seconds). Clean session flag 0x02 is always set in connect-flags when no credentials/will. Credentials: username sets 0x80, password (only when username present) sets 0x40. Will sets 0x04 plus QoS<<3 and retain<<5.

trigger_keywords: what packet identifier does the first subscribe use
how does the packet ID counter start
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The packet identifier counter starts at 1. The CONNECT packet consumes ID 1 internally, so the first SUBSCRIBE or UNSUBSCRIBE request after connecting sends packet identifier 0x00 0x02 (decimal 2). Each subsequent request increments by one.

trigger_keywords: what QoS values are supported for subscribe
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Only QoS 0 and QoS 1 are valid for subscribe. QoS 2 or any higher value (e.g., 254) is rejected: returns false, transmits nothing, outputs 'subscribe=0' and 'sent='.

trigger_keywords: how is the PUBLISH retain flag encoded
difference between retained and non-retained publish
PUBLISH fixed header byte for retained message
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Non-retained PUBLISH: fixed header 0x30. Retained PUBLISH: fixed header 0x31 (bit 0 of flags set). QoS 0 publish has no packet identifier field. The payload follows directly after the topic string (2-byte length prefix + bytes), with no separator or terminator.

Full embedded MQTT client surface: connect success/failure, packet encoding including remaining length and MQTT strings, publish/subscribe/unsubscribe/disconnect packet bodies, inbound PUBLISH decode and callback dispatch, QoS/state transitions, keepalive PINGREQ/PINGRESP timing and timeout disconnect, server availability, and loop-step behavior.

The adapter executes the ordered steps array against the simulated server. Preserve exact packet hex/strings, callback lines, connected state, return codes, and timeout behavior from public_test_cases.

The embedded public_test_cases are part of oracle_data and are authoritative for exact input shapes, action/operation names, stdout line order, whitespace, error categories, and deterministic formatting. When prose and expected_output differ, expected_output is the pass-critical contract.

trigger_keywords: mqtt receive qos1 keepalive oversized stream
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The full receive/loop surface is required: inbound PUBLISH packets must be decoded and delivered to the callback with topic and payload; QoS1 inbound publish must trigger the expected PUBACK packet; oversized messages are dropped or rejected as specified; buffer resizing affects maximum packet size; stream mode emits payload chunks as in public cases; keepalive sends PINGREQ after inactivity, handles PINGRESP, resets on traffic, and disconnects on timeout with the exact state/output lines in feature5 public cases.

trigger_keywords: mqtt remaining length packet id subscribe unsubscribe
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Preserve MQTT remaining-length encoding, UTF-8 string length prefixes, packet id allocation, SUBSCRIBE/UNSUBSCRIBE body encoding, DUP/QoS/retain header bits, and disconnect/reconnect state transitions exactly as the public packet hex outputs require.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the same way the internal packet-identifier counter is managed
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The packet identifier is a simple incrementing counter stored in the client, starting at 1 after construction. It is incremented for each SUBSCRIBE or UNSUBSCRIBE packet sent. The injectable transport abstraction is analogous — it is passed in at construction time as an object implementing read/write/connect/disconnect operations.

context_pointers.vague_reference: default broker port of eighteen eighty three
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Port 1883 (MQTT default). When the 'port' field is omitted from the server object in the adapter input, port 1883 is used. This applies to both {'ip': '...'} and {'host': '...'} endpoint forms.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific information about that constraint. Please refer to the project PRD and the golden implementation under knolleary__pubsubclient/golden/src/ for the authoritative specification.

feature1_1_connect_packets.json.description: A client opens an MQTT session by sending a CONNECT control packet and then waiting for the broker's CONNACK. Given an identifier and optional credentials and/or last-will settings, the client serialises the CONNECT packet on the wire and, on a success CONNACK, reports a connected return and a connected session state. Each case supplies the connection parameters and the broker's CONNACK and observes the exact CONNECT bytes transmitted, the boolean outcome and the resulting session state.

feature1_2_connect_failures.json.description: Connection attempts that cannot complete report failure with a specific session state rather than a generic error. If the underlying transport refuses to open, the client reports a connect-failed state and transmits nothing. If the transport opens but the broker never answers, the client gives up after a fixed timeout window and reports a timeout state. If the broker answers with a non-zero CONNACK return code, the client reports failure and exposes that broker return code as the session state.

feature1_3_disconnect_reconnect.json.description: A connected client can be cleanly disconnected, which transmits a DISCONNECT control packet, closes the transport and returns the session to a disconnected state with no live connection. After disconnecting, the same client can open a fresh session again, transmitting a new CONNECT packet and reaching the connected state on a success CONNACK.

feature2_1_publish.json.description: A connected client publishes an application message to a topic by transmitting a PUBLISH control packet. The packet carries the topic and the raw payload bytes; the payload may be supplied as a text string or as an arbitrary byte sequence (including embedded zero bytes). An optional retained flag is encoded into the packet's fixed-header flags. Each case observes the exact PUBLISH bytes transmitted and the boolean outcome.

feature2_2_publish_rejected.json.description: A publish attempt is rejected (no packet is transmitted and the outcome is false) when the preconditions are not met: publishing on a client that has no live session is refused, and publishing a message whose topic plus payload would not fit in the client's send buffer is refused.

feature3_1_subscribe.json.description: A connected client subscribes to a topic filter by transmitting a SUBSCRIBE control packet carrying an incrementing packet identifier, the topic filter and the requested maximum QoS byte (defaulting to 0). A topic filter is accepted as long as the resulting packet fits in the send buffer. Each case observes the exact SUBSCRIBE bytes transmitted and the boolean outcome.

feature3_2_subscribe_rejected.json.description: A subscribe attempt is rejected (no packet is transmitted, outcome false) when its arguments are invalid or preconditions fail: a requested QoS outside the supported range is refused, subscribing without a live session is refused, and a topic filter too large for the send buffer is refused.

feature3_3_unsubscribe.json.description: A connected client cancels a subscription by transmitting an UNSUBSCRIBE control packet carrying an incrementing packet identifier and the topic filter. Unsubscribing without a live session is refused (no packet transmitted, outcome false).

feature4_1_receive_callback.json.description: While servicing the connection, an incoming QoS 0 PUBLISH packet is parsed and delivered to the application's message handler with the topic (as text), the raw payload bytes and the payload length. Messages up to the buffer's maximum size are delivered in full. Each case feeds inbound PUBLISH bytes, services the connection once and observes the delivered topic, payload and length.

cases.expected_output:
loop=1
message topic=topic payload=70 61 79 6c 6f 61 64 length=7
sent=

feature4_2_receive_qos1.json.description: An incoming QoS 1 PUBLISH packet (which carries a packet identifier) is delivered to the message handler with topic, payload and length, and the client acknowledges it by transmitting a PUBACK control packet echoing that packet identifier. The case observes the delivered message and the PUBACK bytes transmitted.

cases.expected_output:
loop=1
message topic=topic payload=70 61 79 6c 6f 61 64 length=7
sent=40 02 12 34

feature4_3_oversized_dropped.json.description: An incoming PUBLISH packet whose total size exceeds the client's receive buffer is silently dropped: it is not delivered to the message handler. Servicing the connection still succeeds. The case feeds an over-large PUBLISH and observes that no message is delivered.

feature4_4_resize_buffer.json.description: The receive/send buffer can be enlarged at runtime. After growing the buffer to accommodate a larger packet, an incoming PUBLISH that would otherwise be too big is delivered in full to the message handler. The case grows the buffer, feeds a larger PUBLISH and observes the delivered topic, payload and length.

feature4_5_stream.json.description: When the client is configured with an external payload sink (stream), an incoming PUBLISH still delivers the topic and payload length to the message handler, while the payload bytes are streamed out to the sink. In stream mode, payloads larger than the receive buffer are still delivered (topic and length) rather than dropped. Each case feeds inbound PUBLISH bytes and observes the delivered topic and length.

feature5_1_keepalive_ping.json.description: To keep an idle session alive, once the keepalive interval elapses with no traffic the client transmits a PINGREQ control packet when servicing the connection. When the broker answers with a PINGRESP, the outstanding ping is cleared so that, after another idle interval, the client issues a fresh PINGREQ instead of disconnecting. The case advances a virtual clock past the keepalive interval, services the connection, supplies a PINGRESP, then advances again and observes a second PINGREQ.

cases.expected_output:
loop=1
message=none
sent=c0 00
loop=1
message=none
sent=
loop=1
message=none
sent=c0 00

feature5_2_keepalive_timeout.json.description: If a transmitted PINGREQ goes unanswered, the client detects the dead connection: at the next keepalive interval with a ping still outstanding, servicing the connection fails, the transport is closed and the session enters a timeout state. The case sends a ping, withholds any PINGRESP, advances past the next interval, services the connection (which now fails) and observes the timeout state.

cases.expected_output:
loop=1
message=none
sent=c0 00
loop=0
message=none
sent=
state=-4

feature5_3_keepalive_traffic_resets.json.description: Acknowledged inbound traffic refreshes the keepalive window so no spurious ping is emitted. Receiving and acknowledging a QoS 1 PUBLISH updates the activity timers; if the next service occurs within a keepalive interval of that activity, no PINGREQ is transmitted. The case delivers a QoS 1 message before the interval elapses, then services the connection again shortly after and observes that no ping is sent.

cases.expected_output:
loop=1
message topic=topic payload=70 61 79 6c 6f 61 64 length=7
sent=40 02 12 34
loop=1
message=none
sent=
